name: Keyhole Method
description: A transitional 3x3 method that bridges the gap between the beginner method and full CFOP. The keyhole technique uses an empty F2L slot as a 'keyhole' to insert edge pieces that would otherwise be difficult to place. It is faster than the beginner method because you solve the first two layers more efficiently, but it does not require learning full intuitive F2L. Many cubers use keyhole as a stepping stone before committing to F2L.

steps.name: Cross
steps.description: Solve 4 bottom-layer edge pieces, same as CFOP cross. Solve on the bottom so you can see the rest of the cube while working on the next steps.
tips: Same as CFOP cross — plan during inspection

steps.name: First Layer Corners
steps.description: Insert the 4 first-layer corners into their slots. This is the same as the beginner method. Position each corner above its target slot and use R U R' U' (or its mirror) to insert it.
tips: Use R U R' U' trigger to cycle corners into position
Each corner takes 1-5 repetitions of the trigger
Leave one slot empty to use as the keyhole

steps.name: Second Layer Edges (Keyhole)
steps.description: Insert middle-layer edges using the empty slot as a 'keyhole'. Instead of the slow beginner edge insertion algorithms, temporarily remove a first-layer corner, insert the edge piece using simple U + D moves, then replace the corner. This is significantly faster than the beginner's 8-move edge insertions.
tips: Use the empty slot to 'drop in' edges from the top layer
After inserting an edge, put the corner back and move to the next
Much faster than the beginner method for second layer edges
Works well until you learn full intuitive F2L

steps.name: Last Layer
steps.description: Solve the last layer using either the beginner method (4 steps) or 2-look OLL + 2-look PLL (also 4 steps but faster). The keyhole method does not change the last layer at all — use whichever LL approach you know.
tips: Use 2-look OLL/PLL if you know it
Otherwise use the beginner last layer
The keyhole advantage is entirely in the first two layers

pros: Faster than beginner method without learning full F2L
Only requires 2-3 new concepts, not dozens of algorithms
Natural stepping stone to intuitive F2L
Easy to understand — the 'keyhole' concept is intuitive
Can be combined with any last layer approach
cons: Still uses beginner-like first layer corners
Most cubers skip this and go straight to intuitive F2L
creator: Various
